content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 26,
1993
David L.
Pen
PERI,
AIJVARADO
&
FREY
Certified Public Accountants,
Inc.
P.O.
Box 22557
Santa Barbara,
CA
93121
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
A—93-183
Dear Mr. Pen:
This in response to your letter,
which seeks confirmation of
telephone advice that I provided to your office on April 29,
1993.
Your firm is treasurer for Senator Gary Hart’s committee
formed for the purpose of running for the office of Superintendent
of Public Instruction.
A fundraiser was planned for April 27,
1993
and tickets sold for the event.
After the tickets were sold
but before April 27,
1993,
Senator Hart decided not to run for
this office and the fundraising event was cancelled.
You now
desire to return the checks or the monetary equivalent to the
donors who bought tickets for the event.
You asked if this would
be appropriate under the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1
.
As I stated on the telephone on April 29,
1993, it would be
permissible for Senator Hart to use the committee’s funds to repay
persons who bought tickets to this fundraiser.
Since no specific rules govern the return of non—surplus
campaign funds
2
,
we look to Sections 89510 and 89512
of the Act.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 g, g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Compare Section 89519(b),
which requires the pro rata return of
campaign funds to contributors in the event a candidate has left
office or has lost an election for which the funds were raised.
48 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—183
Page
2
Section 89510(b)
states:
All contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office.
.
. that he or she
intends to seek...
Section 89512 states:
An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful
execution of the trust imposed by Section 89510 if
it is reasonably related to a political purpose...
(Emphasis added.)
Furthermore,
Section 89513(f) (1) prohibits the use of
campaign funds to make personal gifts unless the gift is directly
related to a political,
legislative or governmental purpose.
However, this provision goes on to state:
“The refund of a
campaign contribution does not constitute the making of a gift.”
Based on a reading of Sections 89510,
89512
and 89513, it is
clear that campaign funds may be returned to contributors so long
as the return is reasonably related to a political purpose.
Under
the facts you present,
we believe the return of funds collected
for Senator Hart’s April 27,
1993 fundraising is reasonably
related to a political purpose.
The fundraiser was cancelled and
the prospective attendees are merely being given their money back.
At the very least, this is reasonably related to a political
purpose because it assists in maintaining the candidate’s
credibility with his contributors.
A question you have asked that was not posed to me in my
telephone conversation with your office is whether the committee
may return the checks received for the fundraiser without
depositing them into the committee’s bank account.
This is also
permissible so long as the check is returned to the contributor
before the closing date of the campaign statement on which the
contribution would otherwise be reported.
(Section 84211(g).)
Should you have any questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincer ly,
o
Hlarin
Assistant General Counsel
SH:ak

sections.question: Whether it would be permissible under the Political Reform Act for Senator Hart to return checks or monetary equivalent to donors who bought tickets for a cancelled fundraising event after the candidate decided not to run for Superintendent of Public Instruction, and whether the committee may return checks without depositing them into the committee's bank account.
sections.conclusion: Based on a reading of Sections 89510, 89512 and 89513, it is clear that campaign funds may be returned to contributors so long as the return is reasonably related to a political purpose. Under the facts presented, the return of funds collected for Senator Hart's April 27, 1993 fundraising is reasonably related to a political purpose because the fundraiser was cancelled and prospective attendees are merely being given their money back. At the very least, this maintains the candidate's credibility with contributors. The committee may also return checks without depositing them into the committee's bank account so long as the check is returned to the contributor before the closing date of the campaign statement on which the contribution would otherwise be reported (Section 84211(g)).
sections.question_synthetic: Whether campaign funds can be returned to donors for a cancelled fundraising event after a candidate withdraws from a race, and whether such returns can be made without depositing the checks into the campaign account.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that campaign funds can be lawfully returned to ticket buyers for the cancelled fundraiser as it is reasonably related to political purpose and helps maintain candidate credibility. Additionally, checks may be returned without depositing them if returned before the relevant campaign statement closing date.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a confirmation of prior telephone advice. Both a primary question (return of funds) and a secondary question (return without depositing) are addressed. The document explicitly states it is confirming telephone conversation from April 29, 1993. High confidence in extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether it would be permissible under the Political Reform Act for Senator Hart to return checks or monetary equivalent to donors who bought tickets for a cancelled fundraising event after the candidate decided not to run for Superintendent of Public Instruction, and whether the committee may return checks without depositing them into the committee's bank account.

CONCLUSION: Based on a reading of Sections 89510, 89512 and 89513, it is clear that campaign funds may be returned to contributors so long as the return is reasonably related to a political purpose. Under the facts presented, the return of funds collected for Senator Hart's April 27, 1993 fundraising is reasonably related to a political purpose because the fundraiser was cancelled and prospective attendees are merely being given their money back. At the very least, this maintains the candidate's credibility with contributors. The committee may also return checks without depositing them into the committee's bank account so long as the check is returned to the contributor before the closing date of the campaign statement on which the contribution would otherwise be reported (Section 84211(g)).

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms telephone advice that Senator Gary Hart's campaign committee may return funds collected for a cancelled April 27, 1993 fundraiser to ticket purchasers, as such returns are reasonably related to political purpose and do not constitute improper gifts. The committee may also return checks without depositing them if done before the campaign statement closing date.
